✦ The Memory Wall Contents Cheatsheet

A BookBank Field Guide · Computer Architecture

The Memory Wall

Why modern computing is bottlenecked by data, not compute —
chiplets, the memory hierarchy, and the LLM that finally made everyone notice.

Told in the voice of an engineer who still can't help opening the case —
because the inside of the case just changed completely.

Prologue

The lunch stopped being free

For about forty years, you got faster software for free. Every year or two, transistors shrank, clocks climbed, and your code ran quicker without you touching a line of it — the tail end of Dennard scaling and Moore's Law doing the work while engineers slept. That deal quietly ended in the mid-2000s, when clock speeds plateaued and chipmakers pivoted to more cores instead of faster ones. And now, in 2026, we've hit the next wall behind that one: it isn't the arithmetic that's slow anymore — a modern accelerator can multiply matrices faster than almost anything on Earth can feed it numbers. The bottleneck moved. It's data movement: getting bits from memory to the place that does math on them, fast enough and often enough to keep that math unit busy. This book is a tour of why that happened, why it's the defining constraint of the LLM era specifically, and what the best engineers are doing about it.

  1. The Modern CPU Isn't What You ThinkChiplets · P/E cores · 3D stacking
  2. The Memory Hierarchy Is the Real CPUL1–L3 · HBM · DDR5 · HBF
  3. LLM Inference Exposes the BottleneckDecode is memory-bound
  4. Interconnects Are the New BusPCIe · NVLink · UCIe
  5. The Four Fixes Architects Are PursuingHBF · PNM · 3D stacking · new decoding
  6. What This Means for Software EngineersCache-aware code · the road ahead
  7. CheatsheetTerms, the four fixes, a checklist
How to use this book Turn pages with the ← → arrows or the buttons below. Tap ↑ to jump back to this Contents page. Every diagram is drawn, not photographed — it'll render crisp at any size, even on a phone.
